title: SOLID - Single Responsibility Principle
description: A class should have only one reason to change. Each module or class should be responsible for a single part of the functionality.
best_practices: Keep classes focused on one concern

title: SOLID - Open/Closed Principle
description: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification. New functionality should be added through new code, not changing existing code.
anti_patterns: Modifying existing code for new features

title: SOLID - Liskov Substitution Principle
description: Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without breaking the application.
best_practices: Use composition over inheritance when behavior differs

title: SOLID - Interface Segregation Principle
description: Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use. Prefer many small, specific interfaces over one large, general-purpose interface.
anti_patterns: One interface for all use cases

title: SOLID - Dependency Inversion Principle
description: High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions. Abstractions should not depend on details.

title: Clean Architecture
description: Separates software into concentric layers where dependencies point inward. Core business logic is independent of frameworks, UI, database, and external agencies.
best_practices: Separate entities, use cases, interfaces, and frameworks

title: Hexagonal Architecture
description: Also known as Ports and Adapters. Application core defines ports (interfaces) and adapters implement them for different technologies.
best_practices: Define ports for all external interactions

title: Layered Architecture
description: Organizes code into horizontal layers: Presentation, Business Logic, Data Access. Each layer only communicates with adjacent layers.

title: Modular Monolith
description: A monolithic application organized into well-defined, loosely coupled modules. Provides benefits of modularity without distributed system complexity.

title: Event-Driven Architecture
description: Components communicate through events rather than direct calls. Promotes loose coupling and enables reactive systems.

title: Domain-Driven Design
description: Approach to software development that focuses on core domain and domain logic, using a common language between developers and domain experts.

title: Design Patterns - Creational
description: Patterns for object creation: Factory Method, Abstract Factory, Builder, Singleton, Prototype. Help make systems independent of object creation mechanisms.
best_practices: Use Factory for complex object creation
Use Builder for objects with many parameters
Use DI containers over manual factories

title: Design Patterns - Behavioral
description: Patterns for object communication: Strategy, Observer, Command, State, Template Method. Define how objects interact and distribute responsibility.
best_practices: Use Template Method for algorithm skeletons
anti_patterns: Complex if-else chains instead of State

title: Microservices Architecture
description: Structures an application as a collection of loosely coupled, independently deployable services. Each service owns its data and can be developed, deployed, and scaled independently.

title: Test-Driven Development
description: Development methodology where tests are written before production code. Red-Green-Refactor cycle ensures code meets requirements and remains maintainable.
best_practices: Maintain high test coverage for critical paths
